facts.text: KISS became known for their elaborate face paint, pyrotechnics, and blood-spitting stage shows in the 1970s.
Keith Richards of the Rolling Stones developed the open-G tuning style that defined many of the band's greatest riffs.
Joni Mitchell, while primarily a folk artist, profoundly influenced rock musicians with albums like 'Blue' and 'Court and Spark.'
Muddy Waters electrified the blues in Chicago in the late 1940s, laying the groundwork for rock and roll.
The psychedelic rock movement of the late 1960s was characterized by experimental sounds, extended jams, and drug-influenced lyrics.
Weezer's self-titled debut album in 1994 blended power pop with alternative rock, becoming a multi-platinum hit.
Rolling Stone magazine's list of the 500 Greatest Albums of All Time has become the most widely referenced album ranking.
Bon Scott, AC/DC's original lead singer, died on February 19, 1980, and was replaced by Brian Johnson.
The Clash blended rock and roll with reggae, ska, and funk, earning the nickname 'The Only Band That Matters.'
Rock and roll has been declared dead by critics and journalists in every decade since the 1950s, yet it continues to evolve.
The Moody Blues' 'Days of Future Passed' was one of the first rock albums to incorporate a full symphony orchestra.
Genesis began as a progressive rock band under Peter Gabriel before becoming a mainstream pop-rock act under Phil Collins.
Pink Floyd's 'The Wall' concerts featured a massive wall built across the stage during the show.
Garage rock emerged in the mid-1960s as raw, amateur bands played stripped-down rock and roll in suburban garages across America.
Chuck Berry's guitar intro to 'Johnny B. Goode' is considered the most famous opening riff in rock history.
The Beatles' rooftop concert on January 30, 1969, atop Apple Corps in London, was their final public performance.
Freddie Mercury of Queen is widely regarded as one of the greatest rock vocalists of all time.
Soundgarden was one of the first grunge bands to sign with a major label, paving the way for the Seattle scene.
Buddy Holly and the Crickets were one of the first rock bands to use the standard lineup of two guitars, bass, and drums.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame's first class of inductees was announced in 1986.
Dire Straits' 'Money for Nothing' was one of the first music videos to use computer-generated animation on MTV.
Pink Floyd's 'The Dark Side of the Moon' spent 937 weeks on the Billboard 200 chart.
Little Richard's high-energy vocal style and flamboyant stage presence influenced artists from The Beatles to Prince.
The vinyl revival that began in the 2010s has been driven largely by rock music fans seeking a warmer, analog sound.
Stone Temple Pilots were one of the most commercially successful rock bands of the 1990s despite critical skepticism.
The Beatles' 'Let It Be' was their final album released, though 'Abbey Road' was the last one they recorded.
Ironically, ZZ Top's drummer Frank Beard is the only member of the band who does not have a long beard.
Arctic Monkeys became the first band to reach number one in the UK primarily through internet buzz with their debut album in 2006.
The first inductee into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame to have also been a session musician was Hal Blaine in 2000.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame nomination process is overseen by a committee of music historians, industry professionals, and artists.
Southern rock blended rock and roll with country and blues, with bands like Lynyrd Skynyrd and the Allman Brothers Band leading the genre.
George Harrison's 'All Things Must Pass' in 1970 was the first solo album by a Beatle to reach number one.
The Quarrymen, John Lennon's skiffle group, evolved into The Beatles after Paul McCartney and George Harrison joined.
The Cars helped bridge new wave and rock in the late 1970s and early 1980s with their synth-driven sound.
Lollapalooza was created by Jane's Addiction frontman Perry Farrell in 1991 as a touring alternative rock festival.
Rock and roll helped create the concept of the teenager as a distinct cultural identity in the 1950s.
King Crimson's 'In the Court of the Crimson King' in 1969 is considered the first progressive rock album.
Jim Morrison of The Doors called himself 'an erotic politician' and was known for his poetic, provocative lyrics.
Cream, featuring Eric Clapton, Jack Bruce, and Ginger Baker, is considered the first rock supergroup.
The Cure helped define gothic rock in the 1980s with atmospheric guitars and Robert Smith's distinctive appearance.
Captain Beefheart's 'Trout Mask Replica' is one of the most experimental albums in rock history.
David Bowie's alter ego Ziggy Stardust became one of the most famous characters in rock history.
Chuck Berry's signature duck walk became one of the most iconic moves in rock and roll history.
Sting's solo career after The Police combined rock with jazz, world music, and classical influences.
Link Wray's 1958 instrumental 'Rumble' is the only instrumental track ever banned from radio for being too suggestive of violence.
Ian Curtis of Joy Division died on May 18, 1980, the day before the band was to embark on their first American tour.
The talk box, used by Peter Frampton and Joe Walsh, routes guitar sound through a tube into the player's mouth to shape the tone.
The Fillmore venues in San Francisco and New York, run by Bill Graham, became legendary rock and roll concert halls.
Rock musicals like 'Hair,' 'Jesus Christ Superstar,' and 'Rent' brought rock and roll to the Broadway stage.
Sister Rosetta Tharpe, a gospel singer and guitarist from the 1940s, is often called the 'Godmother of Rock and Roll.'
The electric bass guitar, introduced by Fender in 1951, replaced the upright bass in rock bands and became standard.
Hair metal bands of the 1980s, like Mötley Crüe and Poison, were known for big hair, makeup, and anthemic party songs.
The concept of the guitar hero emerged in rock and roll, with players like Hendrix and Clapton worshipped for their virtuosity.
The Rolling Stones' 'Steel Wheels' tour in 1989 set new records for rock concert revenue.
Santana's performance at Woodstock launched their career, blending Latin rhythms with rock guitar.
Bruce Springsteen's 'Born in the U.S.A.' was widely misinterpreted as a patriotic anthem when it was actually a critique of the Vietnam War.
Dick Dale's heavy use of reverb on his Fender guitar defined the surf rock sound of the early 1960s.
Bob Dylan controversially 'went electric' at the Newport Folk Festival on July 25, 1965, shocking folk purists.
Robert Plant and Jimmy Page of Led Zeppelin faced a copyright trial over 'Stairway to Heaven' in 2016 but were found not liable.
Ike Turner's 'Rocket 88,' recorded at Sun Records in 1951, is frequently cited as one of the first rock and roll records.
Glam rock, featuring artists like David Bowie and T. Rex, brought theatricality and androgyny to rock in the early 1970s.
The Rolling Stones took their name from a Muddy Waters song, reflecting rock and roll's deep roots in the blues.
Little Richard's 'Tutti Frutti' was recorded in 1955 and is considered one of the founding songs of rock and roll.
Alice in Chains combined grunge with heavy metal, creating a dark, distinctive sound.
Frank Zappa's eclectic approach combined rock with jazz, classical, and avant-garde music across over 60 albums.
Elvis Presley died on August 16, 1977, at Graceland in Memphis at the age of 42.
Peter Gabriel's solo career pioneered the use of world music influences in rock.
Dick Clark's American Bandstand television show helped popularize rock and roll with mainstream American audiences starting in 1957.
The Rolling Stones' 1972 tour for 'Exile on Main St.' is considered one of the greatest rock tours ever.
Greta Van Fleet drew both praise and criticism for their sound, which closely resembles 1970s Led Zeppelin.
Beck's 'Loser' in 1994 became an unlikely rock hit, blending folk, hip-hop, and noise.
The Ramones' debut album in 1976 featured 14 songs in under 30 minutes, setting the template for punk rock.
The Ventures' 'Walk, Don't Run' was one of the best-selling instrumental rock singles of all time.
Sam Phillips of Sun Records once said he wanted to find a white man who could sing with the feel of a Black artist, leading him to Elvis Presley.
The Doors' Jim Morrison was arrested onstage in Miami on March 1, 1969, for alleged indecent exposure during a concert.
Layne Staley of Alice in Chains had one of the most distinctive voices in rock, combining soul with heavy metal intensity.
U2's 'The Joshua Tree' album, released in 1987, made them one of the biggest rock bands in the world.
The Red Hot Chili Peppers blended funk, punk, and rock into a style that dominated 1990s alternative rock.
Rock and roll's origins lie in a blend of African American blues, gospel, jazz, and boogie-woogie with country and western music.
ZZ Top is known for their long beards, blues-rock sound, and innovative use of synthesizers in the 1980s.
The Foo Fighters' 'Everlong' has been called the greatest modern rock love song.
The Beach Boys' 'Pet Sounds' album influenced The Beatles' 'Sgt. Pepper's' and is considered one of the greatest albums ever made.
Nine Inch Nails' Trent Reznor pioneered industrial rock and later won an Academy Award for film scoring.
The drum solo, a staple of 1960s and 1970s rock concerts, was pioneered by artists like Ginger Baker and Keith Moon.
Queen's 'Bohemian Rhapsody' was groundbreaking for its operatic section and was one of the first true music videos.
Bo Diddley built his first guitar himself from a cigar box, wire, and a wooden board as a teenager.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame requires that an artist's first recording be at least 25 years old for eligibility.
Little Richard announced his retirement from rock and roll multiple times throughout his career, often to pursue gospel music.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inducted its first metal act, Black Sabbath, in 2006.
Rush's '2112' concept album, inspired by Ayn Rand, saved the band from being dropped by their label.
The Beatles broke up in April 1970, with Paul McCartney announcing his departure publicly.
The Black Keys, a two-piece blues rock band from Akron, Ohio, became one of the biggest rock acts of the 2010s.
The Gibson Les Paul guitar, designed with input from guitarist Les Paul, became the preferred instrument for many rock guitarists.
The music press coined the term 'Beatlemania' in 1963 to describe the mass hysteria surrounding The Beatles.
Rock and roll was initially criticized by religious leaders, politicians, and parents who saw it as a threat to morality.
Springsteen's concerts regularly exceed three hours, earning him a reputation as one of rock's most generous live performers.
John Entwistle of The Who was nicknamed 'Thunderfingers' and 'The Ox' for his powerful, innovative bass technique.
The Eagles' 'Hotel California' is one of the most recognizable rock songs ever recorded, known for its twin guitar harmonies.
R.E.M. announced their breakup on September 21, 2011, ending a 31-year career that defined alternative rock.
Ringo Starr's 'It Don't Come Easy' was one of his most successful solo singles, co-produced by George Harrison.
Eddie Cochran's 'Summertime Blues' became an anthem of teenage rebellion when it was released in 1958.
Metallica's 'Black Album' in 1991 brought thrash metal to mainstream audiences, selling over 16 million copies in the U.S.
Vinyl records outsold CDs for the first time since the 1980s in 2020, driven heavily by rock music collectors.
Deadheads, devoted fans of the Grateful Dead, followed the band on tour for years, creating a unique subculture.
Stevie Ray Vaughan revived blues-based rock guitar in the 1980s with his fiery Texas blues style.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame expanded to include artists from hip-hop, pop, and electronic music, sparking debate about the definition of rock.
Woodstock, held in August 1969 in Bethel, New York, drew an estimated 400,000 attendees and became the defining event of the counterculture era.
The Grammy Award for Best Rock Album has been awarded since 1995.
Elvis Presley's Graceland in Memphis, Tennessee, is the second most-visited private home in the United States after the White House.
Butch Vig produced Nirvana's 'Nevermind,' one of the most important rock albums of the 1990s.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame has a 'Singles' category that honors individual songs rather than artists.
CBGB, a club in New York City's Bowery, was the birthplace of American punk rock in the mid-1970s.
System of a Down brought Armenian musical influences into rock and metal, creating a unique sound.
MTV launched on August 1, 1981, with the Buggles' 'Video Killed the Radio Star' as its first music video.
San Francisco's Fillmore Auditorium, under Bill Graham's management, was the epicenter of psychedelic rock in the late 1960s.
The ceremony for the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame was held in Cleveland for the first time in 2009 after previously being held in New York City.
Michael Jackson's 'Thriller' video in 1983 incorporated rock elements and changed the music video format forever.
Brian Epstein, The Beatles' manager, cleaned up their image from leather-clad rockers to suit-wearing pop stars.
The Troggs' 'Wild Thing' became one of the simplest yet most enduring rock songs, built on just three chords.
Rock and roll merchandise, from t-shirts to posters, became a billion-dollar industry by the 1980s.
Neil Peart of Rush was known for his technically complex drumming and for writing most of the band's lyrics.
Gene Vincent's 'Be-Bop-A-Lula' was one of the defining rockabilly recordings of the 1950s.
Prince's 'Purple Rain' album and film in 1984 made him one of the biggest rock and pop stars in the world.
Ahmet Ertegun, co-founder of Atlantic Records, was instrumental in bringing rock, R&B, and soul music to mainstream audiences.
Buffalo Springfield, featuring Neil Young and Stephen Stills, were pioneers of folk rock and country rock.
The term 'rock and roll' was popularized by Cleveland DJ Alan Freed in the early 1950s to describe the rhythm and blues music he played on his radio show.
Def Leppard's 'Pyromania' and 'Hysteria' helped define the sound of 1980s arena rock.
The Isley Brothers' 'Shout' became a staple of rock and roll parties and was later featured in the film 'Animal House.'
The concept album, where songs on a record follow a unified theme, was popularized in rock by The Beatles and The Who.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inducted Whitney Houston in 2020, reflecting the Hall's expanding definition of rock.
PJ Harvey won the Mercury Prize twice, the only artist to do so, for 'Stories from the City' and 'Let England Shake.'
Fats Domino's home in New Orleans was severely damaged during Hurricane Katrina in 2005, and he was initially reported dead before being rescued.
Jack Bruce of Cream helped establish the bass guitar as a lead instrument in rock music.
Joan Jett's 'I Love Rock 'n' Roll' reached number one on the Billboard Hot 100 in 1982 and became an anthem for the genre.
The Byrds pioneered folk rock by electrifying Bob Dylan's songs with jangly Rickenbacker guitars.
Sleater-Kinney became one of the most critically acclaimed rock bands of the late 1990s and 2000s.
Rock festivals like Coachella, Lollapalooza, and Bonnaroo evolved from the tradition established by Woodstock and Monterey Pop.
Electronic effects like chorus, flanger, and phaser became standard tools for rock guitarists in the 1970s and 1980s.
Led Zeppelin played their first concert as a band on October 25, 1968, at the University of Surrey.
Black Sabbath is widely credited with pioneering heavy metal, which grew directly out of hard rock and blues rock.
The 45 RPM single became the primary format for rock and roll music in the 1950s, making music affordable for teenagers.
Jerry Lee Lewis earned the nickname 'The Killer' for his wild, energetic piano-pounding performances.
Brian Wilson of The Beach Boys suffered a mental breakdown while recording 'Smile,' an album that wasn't completed until 2004.
The 'payola' laws passed after the 1950s scandal made it illegal for radio stations to accept payment for playing specific records.
Hendrix's rendition of 'The Star-Spangled Banner' at Woodstock became one of the most iconic performances in rock history.
Stadium rock tours became massive productions in the 1980s, with elaborate lighting, pyrotechnics, and video screens.
The Who's Pete Townshend pioneered the power chord and the windmill strumming technique.
Pearl Jam fought against Ticketmaster's service fees in the mid-1990s, testifying before the U.S. Congress.
The Grateful Dead pioneered the jam band scene, with concerts featuring extended improvisations that could last over 30 minutes.
The riot grrrl movement in the early 1990s, led by bands like Bikini Kill, combined punk rock with feminist activism.
Talking Heads brought art-school intellectualism to rock and new wave, led by David Byrne's eccentric performances.
Sun Records' 'Million Dollar Quartet' session on December 4, 1956, featured Elvis Presley, Jerry Lee Lewis, Carl Perkins, and Johnny Cash.
Peter Frampton's 'Frampton Comes Alive!' was the best-selling live album in the United States until it was surpassed in the 1990s.
The electric guitar solo became a defining element of rock music, with players like Hendrix, Clapton, and Page elevating it to an art form.
The Sex Pistols' 'God Save the Queen' was banned by the BBC but still reached number two on the UK charts.
Oasis and Blur's rivalry in the mid-1990s, known as the 'Battle of Britpop,' dominated UK rock music.
The Yardbirds featured three of rock's greatest guitarists in succession: Eric Clapton, Jeff Beck, and Jimmy Page.
Rock and roll photography became an art form through the work of photographers like Annie Leibovitz, Jim Marshall, and Bob Gruen.
Don Kirshner's Brill Building in New York City was a songwriting factory that produced many early rock and pop hits.
The Beatles' 'Sgt. Pepper's Lonely Hearts Club Band' is frequently cited as the greatest rock album ever made.
Chuck Berry died on March 18, 2017, at the age of 90.
The movie 'Almost Famous' in 2000 depicted the world of 1970s rock journalism and was based on director Cameron Crowe's real experiences.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame opened in Cleveland, Ohio, on September 1, 1995.
The girl group era of the early 1960s, featuring The Supremes and The Shirelles, was closely tied to the rock and roll movement.
Pete Best was The Beatles' original drummer before being replaced by Ringo Starr in August 1962.
The Beatles' 'I Want to Hold Your Hand' was the song that broke them in America, reaching number one in January 1964.
The first Rock and Roll Hall of Fame inductees in 1986 included Chuck Berry, Elvis Presley, James Brown, Little Richard, and Fats Domino.
Chris Cornell's vocal range and emotional intensity made him one of the most acclaimed rock singers of the 1990s.
Dave Grohl played drums for Nirvana, then switched to guitar and vocals to front the Foo Fighters.
The power trio format of guitar, bass, and drums was popularized by bands like Cream and The Jimi Hendrix Experience.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame includes non-performers like record producers, DJs, and music executives in its inductees.
The Beatles held all five top positions on the Billboard Hot 100 chart on April 4, 1964.
The Beatles' 'Abbey Road' album cover, showing the band crossing a street, became one of the most imitated images in pop culture.
Fall Out Boy and Panic! at the Disco helped bring rock to a new generation of fans in the mid-2000s.
George Martin's innovative production techniques for The Beatles included backward tape loops, string quartets, and orchestral arrangements.
The Clash released 'London Calling' on December 14, 1979, an album widely regarded as one of the greatest ever made.
The Velvet Underground's debut album sold poorly but famously influenced nearly everyone who bought it to start a band.
Bob Dylan's 'Like a Rolling Stone' was voted the greatest rock song of all time by Rolling Stone magazine.
Chrissie Hynde of The Pretenders was one of the few women to front a major rock band in the late 1970s and 1980s.
John Bonham of Led Zeppelin is frequently voted the greatest rock drummer of all time in musician polls.
Elvis Presley's 'Heartbreak Hotel' was his first number-one hit on the Billboard pop chart in 1956.
Bruce Springsteen earned the nickname 'The Boss' and became one of rock's greatest live performers.
The Everly Brothers pioneered close harmony singing in rock and roll, influencing The Beatles and Simon & Garfunkel.
Yes's album covers by Roger Dean, featuring fantastical landscapes, became as iconic as the music itself.
The J. Geils Band's 'Freeze Frame' reached number one in 1982, blending rock with R&B energy.
The term 'rockabilly' combines 'rock' from rock and roll with 'hillbilly,' a term for country music.
The Explorer guitar by Gibson, also from 1958, was considered too radical when released and only gained popularity decades later.
The Smiths, led by Morrissey and Johnny Marr, defined indie rock in the 1980s UK.
Elvis Presley made 31 films during his career, though most were formulaic musicals rather than serious dramatic roles.
Prince blended rock, funk, pop, and R&B, and his guitar skills rivaled any rock guitarist of his era.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame's annual induction ceremony is one of the most prestigious events in the music industry.
George Martin, The Beatles' producer, is often called 'the Fifth Beatle' for his crucial contributions to their recordings.
Jimmy Page produced Led Zeppelin's albums and was a pioneer of studio experimentation in rock.
Rick Rubin, co-founder of Def Jam Records, became one of rock's most influential producers, working with artists from Johnny Cash to Red Hot Chili Peppers.
Creedence Clearwater Revival had five top-ten singles in 1969 alone, making them one of the year's biggest rock acts.
The snare drum backbeat on beats two and four is the fundamental rhythmic pattern of rock and roll.
The Band's 'The Weight' is one of the most covered songs in rock history.
REM is credited with pioneering alternative rock in the 1980s, influencing countless indie and college rock bands.
The Isle of Wight Festival in 1970 drew over 600,000 people, making it larger than Woodstock.
The Jeff Beck Group, featuring Rod Stewart on vocals and Ron Wood on bass, was an important bridge between blues and heavy rock.
Elvis Presley's jumpsuits, designed by Bill Belew, became iconic symbols of rock and roll showmanship in the 1970s.
The term 'heavy metal' likely originated from Steppenwolf's 'Born to Be Wild,' which references 'heavy metal thunder.'
The power ballad became a staple of 1980s rock, combining hard rock instrumentation with emotional, slower-tempo songs.
The Rhodes electric piano and Hammond organ became staple keyboard instruments in 1960s and 1970s rock.
The Sex Pistols released 'Anarchy in the U.K.' on November 26, 1976, launching punk rock in Britain.
The New York Dolls' glam punk style in the early 1970s directly influenced both punk rock and hair metal.
The Monterey Pop Festival, held June 16-18, 1967, was the first major rock festival and introduced Jimi Hendrix and The Who to American audiences.
The theremin, one of the earliest electronic instruments, was used by The Beach Boys on 'Good Vibrations' in 1966.
Rock and roll was one of the first genres to unite young audiences across racial lines in America.
Sub Pop Records in Seattle signed Nirvana, Soundgarden, and Mudhoney, becoming the label most associated with grunge.
Elvis Presley's '68 Comeback Special, which aired on December 3, 1968, revitalized his career after years of making movies.
The Edge of U2 pioneered a distinctive guitar sound using extensive delay and echo effects.
The Faces, featuring Rod Stewart and Ron Wood, were one of the most beloved live rock bands of the early 1970s.
Paramore's Hayley Williams became one of rock's most prominent female vocalists in the 2000s and 2010s.
The concert film 'The Last Waltz' documented The Band's final concert in 1976, directed by Martin Scorsese.
King Records in Cincinnati released recordings by James Brown and Little Willie John that bridged R&B and rock.
The invention of the solid-body electric guitar in the late 1940s made rock and roll's loud amplified sound possible.
Jerry Lee Lewis's incendiary performance of 'Great Balls of Fire' at the 1957 Alan Freed show included setting his piano on fire.
Stevie Nicks joined Fleetwood Mac in 1975 and became one of the most iconic women in rock history.
Rickenbacker guitars became associated with the jangly sound of 1960s rock, favored by The Beatles and The Byrds.
Neil Young's long career spans folk, rock, grunge, and electronic music, earning him the nickname 'The Godfather of Grunge.'
Annie Leibovitz's photo of John Lennon and Yoko Ono, taken hours before Lennon's death, is one of the most famous rock photographs ever.
The term 'rock and roll' appeared in print as early as 1934, but it didn't refer to a musical genre until the 1950s.
U2's 'Zoo TV' tour in 1992 used satellite links, multiple video screens, and prank phone calls as part of the show.
The use of feedback as a musical tool in rock was pioneered by The Beatles on 'I Feel Fine' in 1964.
Dion and the Belmonts bridged doo-wop and rock and roll in the late 1950s with hits like 'A Teenager in Love.'
Black Sabbath's Tony Iommi lost the tips of two fingers in a factory accident, which influenced his heavy, detuned guitar style.
The Beatles recorded most of their albums at Abbey Road Studios in London, which became a rock and roll landmark.
Rock and roll's influence extended beyond music into film, fashion, art, and social movements.
Van Halen's Eddie Van Halen revolutionized rock guitar with his two-handed tapping technique.
Fats Domino's laid-back New Orleans piano style influenced rock, R&B, and pop for decades.
The first rock and roll record is heavily debated, with candidates including 'Rocket 88,' 'Rock Around the Clock,' and 'Shake, Rattle and Roll.'
Boston's self-titled debut album in 1976 sold over 17 million copies, driven by Tom Scholz's meticulous guitar production.
Joe Cocker's animated performance of 'With a Little Help from My Friends' at Woodstock became legendary.
The 12-bar blues progression is the harmonic foundation underlying most early rock and roll songs.
Aerosmith's collaboration with Run-DMC on 'Walk This Way' in 1986 bridged rock and hip-hop for the first time.
Jimi Hendrix's 'Purple Haze' introduced psychedelic elements to rock guitar in 1967.
Slash's top hat and Les Paul guitar became defining images of late 1980s rock and roll.
The 'Day the Music Died' refers to February 3, 1959, when Buddy Holly, Ritchie Valens, and The Big Bopper died in a plane crash.
Pearl Jam's 'Ten' and Nirvana's 'Nevermind' were both released in 1991, launching the grunge explosion.
Metallica's concert in Antarctica in 2013 made them the first band to play on all seven continents.
Rock concert lighting evolved from simple spotlights in the 1950s to elaborate computer-controlled laser systems by the 1980s.
Nirvana's 'Nevermind' was released on September 24, 1991, and went on to sell over 30 million copies.
The electric guitar became the defining instrument of rock and roll, replacing the saxophone that dominated early rhythm and blues.
Phil Spector produced hits for The Ronettes, The Crystals, and later worked with The Beatles and the Ramones.
Rock and roll fashion has included leather jackets, ripped jeans, band t-shirts, and studded accessories across decades.
Eddie Vedder of Pearl Jam is known for his baritone voice and for frequently performing while climbing arena rafters in the early 1990s.
Rammstein from Germany became the most commercially successful non-English-language rock band in the world.
Jimi Hendrix's performance at the Monterey Pop Festival in 1967 included setting his guitar on fire onstage.
Marshall amplifiers, first produced in 1962, became synonymous with the loud, distorted sound of rock and roll.
Lynyrd Skynyrd's 'Free Bird' became famous for its extended guitar solo and became a staple of classic rock radio.
The Doors took their name from Aldous Huxley's book 'The Doors of Perception,' which was itself a reference to William Blake.
Elvis Presley's first single, 'That's All Right,' was recorded at Sun Records in Memphis on July 5, 1954.
Taylor Hawkins, Foo Fighters' drummer, passed away on March 25, 2022, in Bogotá, Colombia.
Courtney Love's band Hole brought feminist themes to the forefront of 1990s alternative rock.
Rolling Stone magazine was founded by Jann Wenner in San Francisco in 1967, becoming the leading voice of rock journalism.
Rod Stewart's career spans both rock and pop, with hits ranging from rock anthems to crooner ballads.
John Lennon was shot and killed outside his New York City apartment on December 8, 1980.
Rock and roll tours evolved from small club dates in the 1950s to massive stadium productions by the 1970s.
Sam Cooke bridged gospel, R&B, and rock and roll, and is considered one of the most influential vocalists of the 20th century.
Pete Townshend of The Who was one of the first rock musicians to use guitar feedback and amplifier destruction as part of the performance.
Leo Fender, who designed the Telecaster and Stratocaster guitars, could not actually play the guitar himself.
Tame Impala, primarily the project of Kevin Parker, blends psychedelic rock with electronic production.
Alice Cooper pioneered shock rock with theatrical performances involving guillotines, snakes, and fake blood.
Tool's complex time signatures and philosophical lyrics made them one of the most respected rock bands of the 1990s and 2000s.
The Allman Brothers Band's live album 'At Fillmore East' is considered one of the greatest live rock albums ever recorded.
The first gold record for a rock and roll song was awarded to 'Hard Headed Woman' by Elvis Presley in 1958.
Babymetal from Japan fused heavy metal with J-pop, creating a genre dubbed 'kawaii metal.'
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame building in Cleveland was designed by architect I.M. Pei.
Guitar distortion was first used intentionally by rock musicians in the early 1960s, with Link Wray and The Kinks among the pioneers.
D.J. Fontana was Elvis Presley's drummer and one of the first drummers to play rock and roll on national television.
The White Stripes, featuring Jack and Meg White, led a garage rock revival in the early 2000s.
U2's Bono became as well known for his humanitarian work as for his rock music.
Big Joe Turner's 'Shake, Rattle and Roll' from 1954 is one of the earliest songs considered rock and roll.
The wah-wah pedal, popularized by Jimi Hendrix, creates a vocal-like tone by filtering the guitar signal.
King Gizzard and the Lizard Wizard released five studio albums in 2017 alone, pushing rock prolificacy to new levels.
The longest-running number-one rock song on the Billboard chart is 'Somebody That I Used to Know' by Gotye at 15 weeks.
Jimi Hendrix died in London on September 18, 1970, at the age of 27.
Rage Against the Machine blended rock with hip-hop and politically charged lyrics in the 1990s.
The Vietnam War era saw rock music become a vehicle for protest, with songs by artists like Country Joe and the Fish and Edwin Starr.
Chuck Berry's 'Johnny B. Goode' was included on the Voyager Golden Record sent into space in 1977.
The Moog synthesizer, introduced to rock by artists like Keith Emerson and Rick Wakeman, expanded the genre's sonic palette.
Jerry Wexler of Atlantic Records coined the term 'rhythm and blues' to replace 'race music' in Billboard magazine.
Rock and roll album cover art became an art form in itself, with designs by artists like Roger Dean, Storm Thorgerson, and Andy Warhol.
My Chemical Romance's 'The Black Parade' in 2006 was a rock opera influenced by Queen and Pink Floyd.
Axl Rose of Guns N' Roses has one of the widest vocal ranges in rock music, spanning over five octaves.
Chess Records released seminal recordings by Muddy Waters, Chuck Berry, and Howlin' Wolf that shaped rock and roll.
Eric Clapton was the first person inducted into the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame three times: as a solo artist and as a member of the Yardbirds and Cream.
Phil Spector's 'Wall of Sound' technique used multiple instruments playing the same part to create a dense, layered effect.
Carl Perkins wrote 'Blue Suede Shoes,' one of the first rockabilly hits, which was later covered by Elvis Presley.
Bo Diddley's self-titled debut single introduced the 'Bo Diddley beat,' a syncopated rhythm that influenced countless rock musicians.
Alan Freed's rock and roll concerts in the 1950s were among the first integrated musical events in America.
Wanda Jackson, known as the 'Queen of Rockabilly,' was one of the first women to record rock and roll.
The groupie phenomenon became a notable part of rock culture in the late 1960s and 1970s.
Arena rock emerged in the 1970s as bands like Journey, Foreigner, and Styx filled large venues with anthemic, radio-friendly rock.
Scorpions, a German rock band, scored a worldwide hit with 'Rock You Like a Hurricane' in 1984.
The Who's Keith Moon was known for his wild drumming style and for destroying his drum kit onstage.
The Warped Tour, which ran from 1995 to 2019, was the longest-running touring music festival in North America.
Bob Dylan was awarded the Nobel Prize in Literature on October 13, 2016, partly for his contributions to rock songwriting.
Del Shannon's 'Runaway' in 1961 featured one of the first uses of an electronic keyboard in a rock hit.
Deep Purple was listed in the Guinness Book of World Records as the loudest band in the world in 1972.
The MC5's performance at the 1968 Democratic National Convention protests linked rock and roll with political activism.
The Rolling Stones played their first gig at the Marquee Club in London on July 12, 1962.
The Telecaster, Fender's first solid-body electric guitar, was introduced in 1950 and is still used by rock musicians today.
The Pretenders blended punk attitude with classic rock songwriting, led by Chrissie Hynde's distinctive voice.
Robert Johnson, a Delta blues artist from the 1930s, is considered one of rock and roll's most important musical ancestors.
Brian Eno's ambient production techniques influenced rock bands from U2 to Coldplay.
Nirvana's 'Smells Like Teen Spirit' in 1991 brought alternative rock and grunge into the mainstream.
INXS brought Australian rock to global audiences in the 1980s with Michael Hutchence's charismatic performances.
Ozzy Osbourne was fired from Black Sabbath in 1979 but went on to a highly successful solo career.
NME (New Musical Express) has been one of the most influential rock music publications since the 1950s.
Led Zeppelin's 'Stairway to Heaven' is one of the most played songs in rock radio history, though it was never released as a single.
The 13th Floor Elevators are often credited as the first psychedelic rock band, originating from Austin, Texas.
Queens of the Stone Age's 'Songs for the Deaf' is considered a landmark of 2000s rock.
The Fender Stratocaster, introduced in 1954, became one of the most iconic electric guitars in rock history.
Iron Maiden's mascot Eddie, created by artist Derek Riggs, became one of the most recognizable images in rock.
Van Halen's debut album in 1978 is considered one of the greatest debut rock albums of all time.
Jack White has been a leading figure in preserving and revitalizing rock and roll traditions in the 21st century.
Tom Petty and the Heartbreakers blended rock and roll with folk and country influences across a career spanning four decades.
The Who's 'My Generation' with its stuttered vocals and explosive energy became a generational anthem in 1965.
The guitar riff from The Rolling Stones' '(I Can't Get No) Satisfaction' was played by Keith Richards on a Gibson Maestro fuzz pedal.
Live Aid, held on July 13, 1985, was a dual-venue concert in London and Philadelphia that raised money for Ethiopian famine relief.
The Altamont Free Concert on December 6, 1969, featuring the Rolling Stones, ended in violence and is considered the end of the 1960s idealism.
Pat Benatar won four consecutive Grammy Awards for Best Female Rock Vocal Performance from 1981 to 1984.
The Kingsmen's 1963 version of 'Louie Louie' was investigated by the FBI for two years over allegedly obscene lyrics.
Three members of Lynyrd Skynyrd, including lead singer Ronnie Van Zant, died in a plane crash on October 20, 1977.
The Gibson SG became the signature guitar of Angus Young of AC/DC, who performs in his trademark schoolboy uniform.
Tina Turner's solo career resurgence in the 1980s, starting with 'Private Dancer,' is one of the greatest comebacks in rock history.
Creem magazine, founded in Detroit in 1969, was known for its irreverent rock criticism and coined the term 'punk rock.'
AC/DC's 'Back in Black' is one of the best-selling albums of all time, with over 50 million copies sold worldwide.
Roy Orbison's operatic vocal range and emotional ballads made him unique among early rock and roll artists.
Les Paul, the guitarist and inventor, pioneered multitrack recording technology that became essential to rock production.
Stray Cats revived rockabilly in the early 1980s with hits like 'Rock This Town' and 'Stray Cat Strut.'
Green Day's 'American Idiot' in 2004 was a rock opera that was later adapted into a Broadway musical.
The British Invasion began in 1964 when The Beatles appeared on The Ed Sullivan Show, drawing an estimated 73 million viewers.
Queen's 'We Will Rock You' stomp-stomp-clap rhythm was designed by Brian May so stadium audiences could participate.
The Beatles sold more records worldwide than any other rock band in history, with estimated sales exceeding 600 million.
The Strokes' 'Is This It' in 2001 helped spark a rock revival in New York City.
The jukebox was a major distribution channel for rock and roll in the 1950s, placing music in diners and hangouts across America.
Iggy Pop and The Stooges are considered proto-punk pioneers whose raw, aggressive sound influenced punk rock.
Rock and roll drumming patterns evolved from the basic backbeat of early rock to complex polyrhythms in progressive and art rock.
Radiohead's 'OK Computer' in 1997 was a landmark album that pushed rock music into experimental territory.
Kurt Cobain of Nirvana became the reluctant voice of Generation X before his death on April 5, 1994.
Little Richard was known as the 'Architect of Rock and Roll' for his foundational influence on the genre.
The National became one of the defining indie rock bands of the 2010s with their baritone-voiced, literary approach.
Clapton's 'Layla,' written about George Harrison's wife Pattie Boyd, is one of the most famous rock love songs.
The twelve-string guitar, used by Roger McGuinn of The Byrds, created the distinctive chiming sound of folk rock.
The term 'grunge' was first used in print to describe the Seattle sound by music critic Mark Arm in 1981.
Howlin' Wolf's raw vocal style and powerful stage presence directly influenced many British Invasion bands.
The leather jacket became a symbol of rock and roll rebellion, popularized by Marlon Brando and adopted by musicians.
Elvis Presley's controversial hip-shaking performances on television in the 1950s led censors to film him only from the waist up.
The double-neck guitar, famously used by Jimmy Page, allows a guitarist to switch between six-string and twelve-string parts mid-song.
The Drifters' 'Save the Last Dance for Me' combined rock rhythms with sophisticated pop arrangements.
Jimi Hendrix, Janis Joplin, Jim Morrison, and Brian Jones all died at the age of 27, giving rise to the '27 Club' legend.
Chess Records in Chicago was instrumental in bringing electric blues and early rock and roll to mainstream audiences.
Queen's performance at Live Aid is widely regarded as one of the greatest live rock performances in history.
The Ramones played their first gig at CBGB on August 16, 1974.
Fleetwood Mac's 'Rumours' was recorded while band members were going through painful breakups, fueling its emotional intensity.
The Police blended rock with reggae and new wave influences, becoming one of the biggest bands of the early 1980s.
Scotty Moore, Elvis Presley's guitarist, created the distinctive rockabilly guitar sound heard on Elvis's early Sun Records recordings.
Rock and roll influenced the civil rights movement by bringing Black and white audiences together at concerts and dances.
Eric Clapton earned the nickname 'Slowhand' from audiences who would slow-clap while he replaced broken guitar strings mid-concert.
Lester Bangs was one of the most influential rock critics, writing for Creem and Rolling Stone in the 1970s.
The payola scandal of 1959-1960 exposed radio DJs who accepted bribes to play certain records, leading to Alan Freed's downfall.
Album-oriented rock (AOR) radio format emerged in the late 1960s, playing full albums instead of just singles.
Paul McCartney's band Wings had a string of hits in the 1970s, including 'Band on the Run' and 'Live and Let Die.'
The Smashing Pumpkins' 'Mellon Collie and the Infinite Sadness' was a double album that reached number one in 1995.
Steve Albini, an influential producer and musician, was known for his raw recording style on albums by Pixies and Nirvana.
Wilco's 'Yankee Hotel Foxtrot' was famously rejected by its label, then released to massive critical acclaim on another label.
Heart's Ann and Nancy Wilson were pioneering women in hard rock, combining heavy guitar with powerful vocals.
The British Invasion's second wave included bands like The Hollies, The Dave Clark Five, and Herman's Hermits.
Sun Records, founded by Sam Phillips in Memphis in 1952, launched the careers of Elvis Presley, Jerry Lee Lewis, Johnny Cash, and Carl Perkins.
The transistor radio, introduced in the 1950s, allowed teenagers to listen to rock and roll privately, away from parental supervision.
Blondie's Debbie Harry became one of the first rock stars to bridge punk, new wave, and mainstream pop.
Alan Freed organized the Moondog Coronation Ball in Cleveland on March 21, 1952, often considered the first major rock and roll concert.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame's induction process allows fans to vote alongside a professional committee.
Chuck Berry is often called the 'Father of Rock and Roll' for his influential guitar riffs, showmanship, and songwriting.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame nominated its first hip-hop acts in the 2000s, recognizing rap's roots in rock culture.
Buddy Holly was one of the first rock and roll artists to write, produce, and perform his own material.
The use of the pentatonic scale is one of the defining characteristics of rock guitar soloing.
